flashcards.back: A dependency that occurs across different iterations of a loop, where an iteration i depends on the result of a previous iteration j (j < i). This creates a sequential ordering constraint.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a flow (RAW) and an anti (WAR) dependency?
flashcards.back: Flow (Read-After-Write): Iteration j writes a value that iteration i (i>j) reads. Anti (Write-After-Read): Iteration j reads a value that iteration i (i>j) later overwrites.

flashcards.front: How does the OpenMP `reduction` clause work?
flashcards.back: It creates a private copy of the reduction variable for each thread. Each thread performs the operation on its private copy. At the end of the parallel region, all private results are combined into the shared global variable.

flashcards.front: Why is a `critical` section often bad for performance in a loop?
flashcards.back: It serializes access to the code block it protects. If this block is inside a loop, it forces iterations to run one-at-a-time, eliminating parallelism and adding synchronization overhead.

flashcards.front: Name a common loop pattern that has a loop-carried flow dependency.
flashcards.back: Prefix sum (or cumulative sum): `a[i] = a[i] + a[i-1];` or any recurrence relation where the current value depends on the previous one.
